ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for Bee Hoon Goreng Ikan Bilis; closest defensible match is stir-fried noodle dish (FOODON_03315671). Exact dish-level code not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle dish — stir-fried rice vermicelli with anchovies and vegetables
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (noodle-based dish) used as closest defensible category anchor. No exact FoodEx2 entry for Bee Hoon Goreng Ikan Bilis exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for this specific dish. SNOMED CT does not catalogue regional Southeast Asian noodle preparations at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Bee Hoon Goreng Ikan Bilis weighs approximately 400 g and is a typical single-person serving at a Singaporean hawker centre or Malaysian kopitiam.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore HPB Foodo — Fried Bee Hoon (Mee Hoon Goreng) nutrient composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists fried bee hoon (mee hoon goreng) as the closest approved-source entry. Values for this record are adapted from the HPB Foodo fried bee hoon entry and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) for individual ingredient components (rice vermicelli, anchovies, egg, bean sprouts). The input record's per_100g values (energy 162.5 kcal, protein 6.25 g, fat 7.5 g, carbohydrate 17.5 g) are consistent with the HPB Foodo range for stir-fried bee hoon dishes and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 450 mg/100g is plausible given ikan bilis and soy sauce content and is consistent with HPB data for similar dishes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bee Hoon Goreng Ikan Bilis. GI of 58 is estimated using rice vermicelli (bee hoon) as the primary carbohydrate vehicle. Rice vermicelli GI is reported at approximately 58 (medium) in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), consistent with cooked rice noodle values. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 17.5 × 4.0) / 100 = (58 × 70.0) / 100 = 40.6 g per 400 g plate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from ikan bilis and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: At 450 mg sodium per 100 g, a standard 400 g plate delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium — exceeding 75% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — and individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce, limit ikan bilis quantity, and restrict portion size to no more than 200 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load with medium GI rice vermicelli base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Rice vermicelli carries a medium GI of approximately 58, and a 400 g plate delivers 70 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of approximately 40.6 g; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and monitor postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat with beneficial omega-3 contribution from ikan bilis
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat is relatively low at 2.0 g/100 g and ikan bilis provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids; however, sodium at 450 mg/100 g poses a cardiovascular risk, and individuals with CVD should limit portion size, request reduced soy sauce, and avoid additional high-sodium condiments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 benefit from ikan bilis offset by high sodium and potential nightshade sensitivity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ikan bilis provides anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids and calcium (37.5 mg/100 g) beneficial for RA; however, high sodium (450 mg/100 g) may worsen tissue swelling, and patients sensitive to nightshades should monitor for joint flares from chilli paste ingredients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bee Hoon Goreng Ikan Bilis is a popular Singaporean and Malaysian hawker dish consisting of stir-fried rice vermicelli (bee hoon) cooked in a wok over high heat with crispy dried anchovies (ikan bilis), egg, bean sprouts, spring onion, garlic, shallots, light soy sauce, dark soy sauce, and chilli paste (sambal). The dish is characterised by its savoury, slightly smoky wok-hei flavour, soft yet slightly chewy noodle texture, and the contrasting crunch of the fried anchovies. It is a staple at hawker centres and kopitiams across Singapore and Malaysia and is commonly consumed as a breakfast, lunch, or dinner meal. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 162.5 kcal, 6.25 g protein, 7.5 g fat (of which 2.0 g is saturated), 17.5 g carbohydrate, 2.0 g sugar, 1.25 g dietary fibre, and 450 mg sodium. The ikan bilis contributes calcium (37.5 mg/100 g), iron (0.75 mg/100 g), and omega-3 fatty acids. Clinically, the dish is high in sodium due to the combination of ikan bilis and soy sauce, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The medium glycemic index of the rice vermicelli base (~58) and moderate carbohydrate content require portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is halal-certified in most hawker settings and is dairy-free. It is not suitable for individuals with fish or soy allergies.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Foodo fried bee hoon entry and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for individual ingredients. No exact dish-level entry for Bee Hoon Goreng Ikan Bilis was found in a single approved source; values are consistent with the HPB range for similar stir-fried bee hoon dishes. GI is estimated from the rice vermicelli analogue in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite stir-fried dish is not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central at the dish level as of 2024. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific dish preparation is not reported in HPB Foodo as of 2024. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this dish category as of 2024. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.05 g/100g, consistent with trace levels from vegetable oil used in stir-frying; this is a trace value rather than confirmed zero. All core macros (protein_g 6.25, fat_g 7.5, carbohydrate_g 17.5) are non-zero and consistent with a stir-fried noodle dish containing egg, anchovies, and rice vermicelli. Input ethnic_tags contained irrelevant entries ('Fried Rice', 'Roti Prata', 'Indian') which have been removed; the dish is classified as Singaporean/Malaysian noodle cuisine. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli GI from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008.
